perturbation.original_text: Payment of the Royalty shall be made no later than 45 days after the termination of the preceding full calendar quarter.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment of the Royalty shall be made no later than 90 days after the termination of the preceding full calendar quarter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline for royalties from 45 days to 90 days could violate state wage payment laws if these royalties are considered earned compensation. Many states have stricter deadlines for final wage payments.
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia law requires that all wages or salaries, including commissions, earned shall be paid to an employee upon termination of employment, either by 1) all wages due at the time of termination; or 2) wages shall be paid on the next regular pay day. While the law does not specify a timeframe for independent contractors, delaying payment up to 90 days could be considered unreasonable and may violate implied contract terms for prompt payment, especially if royalties are consistently earned each quarter. Extending to 90 days introduces a potential conflict.

Time and medium of payment; withholding wages; written statement of earnings; agreement for forfeiture of wages; proceedings to enforce compliance; penalties. A. All employers operating a business or engaging an individual to perform domestic service shall establish regular pay periods and rates of pay for employees except executive personnel. All such employers shall pay salaried employees at least once each month and employees paid on an hourly rate at least once every two weeks or twice in each month, except that (i) a student who is currently enrolled in a work-study program or its equivalent administered by any secondary school, institution of higher education, or trade school, and (ii) employees whose weekly wages total more than 150 percent of the average weekly wage of the Commonwealth as defined in § 65.2-500 , upon agreement by each affected employee, may
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the royalty payment deadline from 45 to 90 days, potentially conflicting with state wage payment laws if royalties are considered earned compensation. The provided law snippet (§ 40.1-29) mandates regular pay periods and rates of pay for employees, with salaried employees to be paid at least once a month and hourly employees at least once every two weeks. While the snippet does not directly address royalties or independent contractors, the Law Explanation suggests that delaying payment up to 90 days could be unreasonable and violate implied contract terms, especially if royalties are consistently earned each quarter. The original 45-day period is more likely to align with typical payroll cycles than the extended 90-day period.

perturbation.original_text: Theismann or his representatives, at his cost and expense, shall have the right, upon reasonable notice and during normal business hours, to inspect Bizzingo's books and records and all other documents and material in Bizzingo's possession or control with respect to the determination of Royalties payable hereunder. Theismann shall have free and full access thereto for such purposes and may make copies thereof. All books and records relative to Bizzingo's obligations hereunder shall be maintained and made accessible to Theismann for inspection at a location in the United States for at least one year after termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Theismann or his representatives, at his cost and expense, shall have the right, upon reasonable notice and during normal business hours, to inspect Bizzingo's books and records and all other documents and material in Bizzingo's possession or control with respect to the determination of Royalties payable hereunder. Theismann shall have free and full access thereto for such purposes and may make copies thereof. All books and records relative to Bizzingo's obligations hereunder shall be maintained and made accessible to Theismann for inspection at a location in the United States for at least six months after termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period for royalty-related documents from one year to six months after the contract's termination could conflict with IRS guidelines regarding record-keeping for tax purposes.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 6001 states that every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records, render such statements, make such returns, and comply with such rules and regulations as the Secretary may from time to time prescribe. While not directly mandating a retention period for contracts, the IRS generally recommends retaining records relating to tax liabilities for at least three years from the date of filing the relevant tax return, and sometimes longer. Reducing the contractual retention period to six months limits Theismann's ability to verify royalty payments for tax compliance.

Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records, render such statements, make such
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record retention period from one year to six months, which contradicts the general IRS recommendation to retain records relating to tax liabilities for at least three years. Although 26 U.S. Code § 6001 does not specify a concrete retention period, it mandates that persons liable for tax must keep records and comply with regulations prescribed by the Secretary. The reduced retention period in the changed text could hinder Theismann's ability to comply with IRS regulations regarding tax compliance and verification of royalty payments beyond the six-month mark, as it restricts his access to necessary records.

perturbation.original_text: Bizzingo agrees that any use of the Property for advertising, promotional or sale purposes will be approved in advance by Theismann. Theismann agrees that such material, submitted for approval as provided herein may be deemed by Bizzingo to have been approved hereunder if the same is not disapproved by the Theismann in writing within fourteen (14) days after Theismann's receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Bizzingo agrees that any use of the Property for advertising, promotional or sale purposes will be approved in advance by Theismann. Theismann agrees that such material, submitted for approval as provided herein may be deemed by Bizzingo to have been approved hereunder if the same is not disapproved by the Theismann in writing within three (3) days after Theismann's receipt thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the approval timeframe for advertising materials from 14 days to 3 days may be considered an unreasonably short period, potentially violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing depending on the complexity of the materials and industry standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. While this applies primarily to the sale of goods, the principle of good faith extends to other contracts. Setting an unreasonably short approval period (3 days) could be seen as acting in bad faith if it does not allow Theismann a reasonable opportunity to review and approve materials, especially if they are complex or require legal review. This is circumstantial, but the change introduces the possibility of a breach of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the timeframe for approval from 14 to 3 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this might violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation states that UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' If 3 days is deemed an unreasonably short time, it may constitute a breach of this obligation. This falls under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition because the time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements; specifically, the requirement of good faith.
